FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from U.S. ABCD Study Year 2 assessment (2018-2020), N=9,008 adolescents ages 10-14

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis; linear regression models conducted to examine associations between problematic social media use and alcohol expectancies

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 (phone/SM-specific estimate): SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire) score paired with alcohol expectancies (individual-trait outcome). Model 3: problematic social media use B=0.045 (95% CI 0.020-0.069) for positive expectancies and B=0.072 (95% CI 0.043-0.101) for negative expectancies. Other side is individual-trait (behavioral/cognitive beliefs about alcohol effects).

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression coefficients (β) with 95% confidence intervals reported in Table 2; positive and negative expectancies outcomes

FT_IC4.evidence: Linear regression analysis with quantitative results (β coefficients) for SM-expectancies associations

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal studies. No longitudinal SM-as-DV-only condition met.
notes: Decision: INCLUDE. All criteria met. Cross-sectional analysis of SMAQ (phone/SM variable) and alcohol expectancies (individual-trait) with numeric results.

FT_IC1.evidence: Prospective data from U.S. ABCD Study, Baseline and Year 2 (2018-2020), N=9,673 screen-using adolescents ages 11-12

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis; generalized logistic mixed effects models and generalized linear mixed effects models examining associations between ACEs and problematic screen use

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 (phone/SM-specific estimates): VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire), and MPIQ (Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire) paired with ACEs (individual-trait-level predictor: trauma/adversity exposure). Table 2 adjusted model: problematic video game use OR=3.11 (95% CI 2.05-4.73) for ≥4 ACEs; problematic mobile phone use OR=1.57 (95% CI 1.22-2.03) for ≥4 ACEs. Qualifies as phone/SM variables.

FT_IC3.evidence: Odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals reported in Table 2; binary and continuous outcome scales

FT_IC4.evidence: Logistic and linear mixed effects models with quantitative results (OR and continuous scale coefficients)

FT_EC7_reason: All three EC7 conditions met: (a) longitudinal design (baseline → Year 2 follow-up), (b) SM/phone variables (VGAQ, SMAQ, MPIQ) are DV in analyses shown, (c) no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere in paper. Cross-sectional carve-out does not apply (no cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis present). Paper excluded under FT-EC7.
notes: Decision: EXCLUDE (FT-EC7). Longitudinal SM-as-DV-only study with no SM-as-IV analysis.

FT_IC1.evidence: Introduction to special issue describing ABCD Study and papers analyzing U.S. ABCD data

FT_IC5.evidence: Editorial introduction/overview article, not an original empirical analysis. Describes ABCD consortium research and summarizes other papers in the special issue without reporting new empirical findings or analyses.

notes: Decision: EXCLUDE (FT-EC3). Non-empirical paper (editorial introduction to special issue).
